text: Hi, I'm Vahag Nicolian from OHSU, and this is a video of a robotic tap inguinal hernia repair in a patient who presented with a giant inguinal scrotal hernia.
This patient was actually referred to me by my urology colleagues. He had presented to their clinic with a right renal mass that needed a partial nephrectomy.
And the CT scan for workup was noted to have a large hernia He was a seventy-nine-year-old gentleman with a history of significant dementia, surgical history for open appendectomy.

After discussing with my neurology colleagues, we elected to actually have him undergo his robotic right partial nephrectomy, which identified a benign growth, and then we did a staged hernia repair for essentially a reducible hernia.
He had a CT scan. You'll note a very large hernia on the left side. and then a hernia on the right side that was essentially made up of preperitoneal fat through the direct and indirect spaces.

In this particular case, I had a very thorough discussion with the patients and his family. His medical decision maker, who was his wife, wanted to avoid a bilateral repair as his contralateral side had no symptoms.
The video shows a large amount of viscera within the left inguinal hernia, with a visible defect. The bladder is not involved in this hernia.
And so we proceeded with a minimally invasive approach with a low threshold to convert to hybrid. Induction of anesthesia, we were able to reduce the majority of his hernia.
During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon identifies an incarcerated portion of the colon, which is manually reduced. A large defect is visible through the deep inguinal ring.

The robotic-assisted surgery begins with the development of a peritoneal flap, followed by preperitoneal dissection. The surgeon proceeds with medial dissection towards the retropubic space and then continues with lateral dissection.
The surgeon encounters adhesive bands between the colon and the hernia sac, opting to minimize dissection in this area. Some dissection appears to have been performed prior to this clip.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection, identifying the location of the pubis, and then shifts focus laterally to transition into the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon performs an initial dissection, revealing a very thin peritoneum. The surgical field shows the sac, with considerations for potential sac reduction based on its difficulty.

I personally think for very large hernias like this, if the sac has significant adhesions in the canal and in the scrotum, that the hematomas that can result from excessive dissection and tissue trauma are not worth it.
The surgeon inverts the hernia sac using gentle counter tension at the bedside, facilitating further dissection and improving orientation within the surgical field.

The surgeon addresses small fenestrations in the redundant sac while encountering adhesions. The decision is made to abandon the sac, and the surgeon proceeds to lice several adhesions, revealing a pseudosac.
The surgeon establishes a clear view of the myopectenial orifice, indicating progress towards the final stages of the operation.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks and creates a pocket for an extra-large heavyweight mesh. Initial retropubic dissection is performed, along with parietalization of structures and exploration of portions of the hernia sac.
The surgeon positions an extra large heavyweight mesh over the hernia defect in a patient of short stature. The mesh is centered over the defect and fixated at several points.

Other things we actively thought about was drains, but we elected not to do so given his dementia and his confusion. And so for this particular case, at the end of it, we basically had him in compression shorts and compression undercardaments, and he did great without any seromas of consequence.

The surgeon closes the superior flap with ease due to the redundancy in the peritoneum. The hernia sac appears very redundant, and a visible fenestration is noted, which will be straightforward to address.
The surgeon placates the hernia sac, carefully closing the opening to prevent inversion and potential obstruction, while minimizing the sac's size to avoid complications.

The surgeon aggressively integrates the mesh into the peritoneum, using stitches to eliminate dead space and plicate the hernia sac. The stitching technique appears random, covering the area thoroughly.

Again, this patient was kept overnight for observation, did wonderful, required no narcotics, we placed no drain, we just did compression undergarments, just given his medical history, and he did remarkably well. I think for any patient with an inguinal scleral hernia, you can approach these robotically or MIS. I think there has to be some branch points regarding an open or hybrid approach, especially for larger defects that may need anterior application. Drains can be used selectively. We didn't use it in this case, but definitely low threshold to use one. And in scenarios where there's significant adhesions between the sac And the canal, it's likely worthwhile to consider abandonment of the sac to avoid complications like hematomas. Thank you very much.
